summary: Julia Evans announces her third 'Bite Size' zine focused on networking, explaining that it covers practical skills like inspecting SSL certificates, making DNS queries, and capturing network traffic. She frames the zine as the resource she wished she had when she started doing networking work, noting that these tasks aren't inherently hard but are obscured by unhelpful man pages. She also reflects on the positive experience of switching from free to paid zines, discusses wanting better feedback from readers, and teases potential future topics.
key_points: Networking tools like tcpdump aren't inherently difficult, but their man pages fail to distinguish essential options from obscure ones, making them intimidating
The zine targets people who need to debug networking problems (SSL errors, DNS updates, firewall issues) even though networking isn't their full-time job
Switching from free to paid zines has been entirely positive: better sales, people take the work more seriously, and quality has improved
She wants more specific, actionable feedback beyond general praise to improve future zines
Practice is the key differentiator in networking skill — spending time debugging weird networking things is how you get better
money_quotes: As usual this zine is the resource I wish I had 4 years ago.
As often happens with computers none of them are really that hard!!

The man pages for the tools you need to do these things are Very Long and as usual don't differentiate between 'everybody always uses this option and you 10000% need to know it' and 'you will never use this option it does not matter'.
So I spent a long time staring sadly at the tcpdump man page.
It's Thursday afternoon and your users are reporting SSL errors in production and you don't know why.
When you practice something you get better at it :)
Sales have been really good, people take the work more seriously, I can spend more time on them, and I think the quality has gone up.

When I made the switch about a year ago from 'every zine I release is free' to 'the old zines are free but all the new ones are not free' it felt scary! It's been startlingly totally fine and a very positive thing.
key_insight: Networking tools aren't inherently hard — the real barrier is that documentation doesn't distinguish the essential from the obscure, making practical knowledge feel more inaccessible than it actually is.
